skipped.heading: Context
skipped.reason: pure background narrative, not a durable mechanism

skipped.heading: audit finding tables
skipped.reason: status tables of findings/fixes; bodies extracted separately

skipped.heading: Where this connects to other ADRs

drafts.title: Sediment commit path is never blocked by git network operations
drafts.compiledTruth: Push is fire-and-forget from sediment's gitCommit. Sediment returns the commit SHA immediately; push runs detached in background. This guarantees agent_end latency is O(seconds), not O(network). Push failures are audited and retried on the next sediment commit, never surfaced to the caller. The invariant holds even when the network is flaky — the audit log + /abrain status ahead count are the user's visibility into push health, not blocking calls.

drafts.title: Cross-device sync uses only deterministic git operations, never LLM merge
drafts.compiledTruth: Abrain cross-device sync uses only git's built-in deterministic merge: fast-forward when behind-only, git 3-way auto-merge when both sides have unique commits on disjoint files. LLM-based merge of markdown bodies is explicitly rejected — a single hallucinated sentence pollutes the knowledge substrate and is hard to detect post-hoc. True textual conflicts (overlapping edits in the same region of the same file) abort with merge --abort restoring the clean working tree, and surface a runbook to the user. No silent resolution, no LLM fabrication.

drafts.title: All git sync operations return typed events, never throw to caller
drafts.compiledTruth: Every git sync operation (pushAsync, fetchAndFF, sync, getStatus) catches all errors internally and returns a typed GitSyncEvent with a result enum: ok, noop, skipped, diverged, conflict, push_rejected, timeout, failed. No exception ever propagates to the caller. This replaces sediment's coarse try-catch around gitCommit and ensures downstream UX (status command, footer hints) always has a structured signal to consume regardless of git subprocess outcome.

drafts.title: Git credentials never pass through process argv, only credential helper or SSH agent
drafts.compiledTruth: Only branch names and standard git verbs pass through execFile. Credentials (tokens, passwords) live exclusively in git's credential helper or SSH agent — neither passes through the sync module's process boundary. This is the argv-in half of credential hygiene. The output-side counterpart (redactCredentials on stderr/stdout captured into audit logs and UI) closes the asymmetry where argv-in was protected but stderr/stdout-out were not.

drafts.title: Concurrent in-process git ops serialize via tail-chained promise queue
drafts.compiledTruth: Multiple sediment writes during one agent_end can trigger near-simultaneous pushAsync calls. Without serialization, the second push fails with .git/index.lock contention. The solution is a tail-chained promise queue: each caller attaches to the tail via tail.then(fn, fn), ensuring strictly sequential execution. A simple inflightOp slot has a TOCTOU race with 3+ concurrent callers (multiple microtasks resume simultaneously after prior inflight resolves), so the tail chain is necessary. Cross-process protection relies on git's own index.lock as fail-soft: one wins, the other audits result=failed and retries on next op.

drafts.title: When no git remote is configured, all sync ops are silent no-ops
drafts.compiledTruth: When hasGitRemote is false (no git repo or no origin remote), every sync op returns result=skipped and writes an audit row, but emits zero console output. No warnings, no errors, no startup messages about sync being unavailable. Users without a configured remote see no noise. This preserves INV-INVISIBILITY for users who never set up a remote — auto-sync is an opt-in via git remote configuration, not a mandatory feature that nags the unconfigured.

drafts.title: Git output captured in audit logs and UI must be credential-redacted
drafts.compiledTruth: Anything captured from git — remote URL from git remote get-url origin, push/fetch stderr embedded in error messages — must pass through redactCredentials() before storage in ~/.abrain/.state/git-sync.jsonl or display in /abrain status. URLs like https://user:token@host/repo become https://***@host/repo. This closes the asymmetry where argv-in was protected (no secrets in argv) but stderr/stdout-out were not, discovered in Round 2 audit.

drafts.title: Divergence runbook paths must be shell-quoted to prevent paste-time code execution
drafts.compiledTruth: The /abrain sync divergence runbook contains cd <abrainHome> for the user to copy/paste. abrainHome must be shell-quoted via shellQuotePath() so a maliciously-crafted ABRAIN_ROOT env cannot become a paste-time code-execution gadget. Control-char paths return a refusal placeholder. This is defense-in-depth: ABRAIN_ROOT is normally set by pi, not user input, but the paste vector crosses a trust boundary.

drafts.title: Auto-merge on divergence when files are disjoint via git 3-way merge
drafts.compiledTruth: The 2026-05-15 design treated any divergence (ahead>0 && behind>0) as requiring manual intervention. The 2026-05-17 revision recognizes that abrain's content shape (one markdown file per slug) means two devices diverging typically edit disjoint files, which git's 3-way merge resolves trivially. New policy: attempt git merge --no-edit --no-ff on divergence with a dedicated abrain-autosync author identity and commit.gpgsign=false. If clean, result=ok with merged count. If textual conflict, merge --abort restores clean tree, result=conflict with conflictPaths, runbook fires. The old diverged result is deprecated but kept in the type union for historical audit parsing.

drafts.title: LLM-based merge of knowledge entries is rejected due to hallucination risk
drafts.compiledTruth: Using an LLM to merge conflicting markdown bodies in abrain carries unacceptable hallucination risk. A single fabricated sentence pollutes the knowledge substrate and is hard to detect post-hoc — there is no cheap automated audit that catches a subtly wrong merged fact. The user explicitly chose manual conflict resolution over LLM merge. Git's own 3-way text merge (same algorithm used for code for decades, no LLM in the loop) is the ceiling for automation. When git itself cannot resolve overlapping edits in the same region, the fallback is the user — never an LLM.

drafts.title: Rebase on divergence is rejected because it silently rewrites local commit SHAs
drafts.compiledTruth: Auto-rebase on divergence would silently rewrite local commit SHAs. Users who manually cd ~/.abrain && git log would see commits move around — the commit they pointed at yesterday is no longer reachable under the same SHA. For a knowledge substrate, git merge --no-ff is preferred because it preserves local SHAs verbatim and adds one explicit merge commit, which is the truthful record of two devices' thoughts converging. Knowledge substrate integrity over convenience. The merge commit IS the story.

drafts.title: Git subprocesses must run with LANG=C for reliable stderr-based error classification
drafts.compiledTruth: Git error messages are locale-dependent. classifyError regex patterns that detect push rejection, conflict markers, and timeout signals must operate on English stderr to avoid misclassification. Every git subprocess (push, fetch, merge, status, diff) runs with GIT_ENV = { LANG: 'C', LC_ALL: 'C', GIT_TERMINAL_PROMPT: '0' } threaded into every execFileAsync call site. Without this, non-English LANG would cause real push rejections to be silently mislabeled as generic failed, and conflict detection via CONFLICT stderr pattern matching would break.
